docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
This tutorial provides a practical introduction to running CLM-FATES on the Hydra HPC cluster at VUB.

The material is based on a combination of practical sessions from the Land Climate Dynamics course (VUB) and the FATES tutorial held at Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory in 2024.

All tutorial material is available on GitHub:

  • CLM-FATES4UGent

Additional resources:

  • FATES Tutorial
  • UGent HPC Introduction

docs.title: What is FATES?
docs.text:
FATES (Functionally Assembled Terrestrial Ecosystem Simulator) is the vegetation demographic model used within land surface models such as ELM and CLM.

FATES represents vegetation as Plant Functional Types (PFTs), which are groups of species sharing similar ecological traits.

The landscape is represented as a mosaic of patches in different successional stages. Each patch contains cohorts of trees of different sizes and PFTs. This allows FATES to simulate competition, disturbance, recruitment, mortality, and succession.

FATES can be run in many different configurations depending on the scientific question:

  • Global simulations
  • Regional simulations
  • Site-level simulations
  • Inventory-initialised simulations
  • Bare-ground spinups
  • Restart simulations

In this tutorial we will first perform a standard CLM-FATES simulation and then move towards site-level simulations driven by local inventory and meteorological data.

docs.title: Tutorial Structure
docs.text:
1. Installation and environment setup
  2. Running a default CLM-FATES simulation
  3. Exploring input data and source code
  4. Running and analysing model output
  5. Site-level simulations
  6. Inventory initialisation
  7. Regional simulations
  8. Modifying FATES parameter files
  9. Further resources and documentation

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: Proceed to the Installation section using the navigation menu on the left.

docs.title: Part 2: Creating and Running a Default CLM-FATES Simulation
docs.text:
CESM simulations follow four main steps:

  1. Create a case
  2. Set up the case
  3. Build the model executable
  4. Submit the model run

These four commands form the core CESM workflow:

./create_newcase
./case.setup
./case.build
./case.submit

docs.title: Understanding create_newcase
docs.text:
The create_newcase command defines:

  • The case name
  • The model resolution
  • The component set (compset)
  • The compiler
  • The target machine

General syntax:

./create_newcase \
    --case CASE_NAME \
    --res RESOLUTION \
    --compset COMPSET \
    --compiler COMPILER \
    --machine MACHINE

docs.title: Case Name
docs.text:
The --case argument defines where the case will be created.

For this tutorial:

--case $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/cases/control

The case will be called:

control

and all case configuration files will be stored under:

$VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/cases/control

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text:
The --res argument defines the model grid.

For this tutorial:

--res f19_g17

This corresponds to a global grid with approximately:

1.9° latitude × 2.5° longitude

Many additional grids are available:

http://www.cesm.ucar.edu/models/cesm2/config/grids.html

docs.title: Compset
docs.text:
The --compset argument defines:

  • Which model components are active
  • Physical parameterisations
  • Forcing configuration
  • Carbon and vegetation options

For this tutorial we use:

I2000Clm50Fates

This is a CLM5-based FATES simulation representing year-2000 conditions.

A full list of CESM compsets is available at:

http://www.cesm.ucar.edu/models/cesm2/config/compsets.html

docs.title: Create the Case
docs.text:
Move to the CESM scripts directory:

cd $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/sources/ctsm-5.2.0/cime/scripts

Create the case:

./create_newcase \
    --case $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/cases/control \
    --res f19_g17 \
    --compset I2000Clm50Fates \
    --compiler gnu \
    --machine hydra \
    --run-unsupported

After completion, move to the case directory:

cd $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/cases/control

Inspect the generated files:

ls

The newly created case now contains all configuration files required to set up, compile, and run the model.

docs.title: Part 3: Configuring the Case with case.setup
docs.text:
Once the case has been created, the next step is to configure how the simulation will run.

The case configuration is stored in a collection of XML files located in the case directory:

cd $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/cases/control

List the contents:

ls

You should see files such as:

env_run.xml
env_batch.xml
env_build.xml
env_mach_pes.xml

These files control:

  • Run length
  • Start date
  • Restart frequency
  • Walltime
  • Number of processors
  • Compilation settings
  • Batch submission options

docs.title: Inspect the Default Run Settings
docs.text:
Open the run configuration file:

vi env_run.xml

Try to find:

  • The simulation start date
  • How long the model will run
  • Restart settings

Although these files can be edited directly, CESM recommends using the xmlchange utility.

docs.title: Disable Short-Term Archiving
docs.text:
For tutorial runs we disable the short-term archiver.

Run:

./xmlchange DOUT_S=FALSE

Verify the change:

./xmlquery DOUT_S

Expected output:

DOUT_S: FALSE

docs.title: Set Simulation Length
docs.text:
For this tutorial we will run a 12-month simulation.

Configure the run length:

./xmlchange STOP_N=12,STOP_OPTION=nmonths

This tells CESM:

STOP_OPTION = nmonths
STOP_N      = 12

meaning:

Run for 12 months.

Verify:

./xmlquery STOP_N
./xmlquery STOP_OPTION

docs.title: Configure Computational Resources
docs.text:
Set the number of MPI tasks and walltime:

./xmlchange NTASKS=4,JOB_WALLCLOCK_TIME=24:00:00

This requests:

4 MPI tasks
24 hours walltime

Verify:

./xmlquery NTASKS
./xmlquery JOB_WALLCLOCK_TIME

docs.title: Run case.setup
docs.text:
Once the configuration is complete, initialise the case:

./case.setup

This command:

  • Generates component namelists
  • Creates batch scripts
  • Creates user namelist files
  • Prepares the case for compilation

New files will appear in the case directory.

Inspect the directory again:

ls

Pay particular attention to:

user_nl_clm
user_nl_datm
CaseDocs/
Buildconf/

These files are generated during setup and contain the final configuration that will be passed to the model.

docs.title: Modifying CLM Namelists
docs.text:
Many CLM options can be changed through:

vi user_nl_clm

This file allows users to override default namelist values.

Examples include:

  • Output variables
  • Surface datasets
  • Initial conditions
  • Carbon cycle options
  • FATES settings

Reference:

https://www.cesm.ucar.edu/models/cesm2/settings/current/clm5_0_nml.html

docs.title: Part 4: Building the Model with case.build
docs.text:
After setup, the model must be compiled.

Compilation converts the source code into an executable that can run on Hydra.

docs.title: Start Compilation
docs.text:
From the case directory:

./case.build

Compilation may take several minutes.

During the build process CESM:

  1. Generates final namelists
  2. Compiles supporting libraries
  3. Compiles individual model components
  4. Links everything into a single executable

docs.title: What Gets Compiled?
docs.text:
For a CLM-FATES run you will typically see components such as:

datm
clm
mosart
cpl

along with supporting libraries:

gptl
mct
pio
csm_share

Briefly:

gptl Timing and profiling library.

mct Model Coupling Toolkit used for communication between components.

pio Parallel I/O library for reading and writing NetCDF files.

csm_share Shared CESM infrastructure used by multiple components.

datm Data atmosphere model providing meteorological forcing.

clm Community Land Model containing FATES.

mosart River routing model.

cpl The coupler that exchanges information between components.

docs.title: Build Success
docs.text:
The build is successful when you see:

MODEL BUILD HAS FINISHED SUCCESSFULLY

Example:

Total build time: 334 seconds
MODEL BUILD HAS FINISHED SUCCESSFULLY

At this point the executable has been created and the model is ready to run.

docs.title: Part 5: Exploring the Source Code and Input Data
docs.text: While the model is compiling it is useful to explore the source code and datasets used by CLM-FATES.

docs.title: Exploring the FATES Source Code
docs.text:
Move to the FATES source directory:

cd $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/sources/ctsm-5.2.0/src/fates

One useful file to inspect is:

cd biogeophys

vi FatesPlantHydraulicsMod.F90

This module contains parts of the plant hydraulics implementation.

The FATES source tree is organised into modules covering topics such as:

allometry
carbon cycling
recruitment
mortality
competition
radiation
hydraulics
disturbance

Do not modify any source files during the tutorial.

docs.title: Exploring Input Data
docs.text:
The CESM input datasets are stored under:

cd $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/inputdata

For CLM-FATES, important datasets include:

surface datasets
domain files
meteorological forcing
parameter files
initial conditions

docs.title: Surface Datasets
docs.text:
Navigate to:

cd $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/inputdata/lnd/clm2/surfdata_esmf

Surface datasets contain information such as:

  • Land cover
  • Soil properties
  • Topography
  • PFT distributions
  • Urban fractions
  • Crop fractions

docs.title: Parameter Files
docs.text:
Navigate to:

cd $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/inputdata/lnd/clm2/paramdata

These files contain:

  • Plant traits
  • Photosynthesis parameters
  • Hydraulic parameters
  • Carbon cycle coefficients
  • FATES PFT definitions

docs.title: Inspect NetCDF Files
docs.text:
Useful commands:

Display metadata:

ncdump -h filename.nc

Display dimensions and variables:

ncks -m filename.nc

Visualise interactively:

ncview filename.nc

(Note that ncview generally requires a graphical session and may not work through the web interface.)

docs.title: Understanding Input Data Requirements
docs.text:
CLM-FATES requires several categories of input data:

Meteorological forcing
Surface datasets
Domain files
Parameter files
Initial condition files

These files determine:

  • Where the model runs
  • Which vegetation is present
  • How the vegetation behaves
  • What climate it experiences
  • How the simulation is initialised

In later sections we will replace many of these default datasets with site-specific FATES inputs.

docs.title: Part 6: Running the Model and Inspecting Output
docs.text: Once the model has been successfully built, it can be submitted to the scheduler.

docs.title: Submit the Model Run
docs.text:
From the case directory:

cd $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/cases/control

Submit the run:

./case.submit

CESM will:

  • Verify required input files exist
  • Generate final namelists
  • Create batch submission scripts
  • Submit the job to SLURM

docs.title: Monitor Job Status
docs.text:
Check the queue:

mysqueue

or

squeue -u $USER

Typical job states include:

PD = Pending
R  = Running
CG = Completing

docs.title: Cancel a Job
docs.text:
If necessary:

scancel <jobid>

Example:

scancel 6031806

docs.title: Locate Model Output
docs.text:
Once the run has completed, move to the run directory:

cd $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/output/control/run

List the contents:

ls

You should see various NetCDF output files.

Typical files include:

control.clm2.h0.0001-01.nc
control.clm2.r.0002-01-01-00000.nc
control.cpl.hi.0001-01.nc

docs.title: Inspect CLM Output
docs.text:
Open the main CLM history file:

ncview control.clm2.h0.0001-01.nc

Alternatively inspect metadata:

ncdump -h control.clm2.h0.0001-01.nc

Questions to explore:

  • Which time period does the file cover?
  • Which variables are available?
  • Which dimensions are present?
  • What units are used?

docs.title: Common Output Variables
docs.text:
Examples include:

GPP
NPP
TLAI
TOTVEGC
TOTSOMC
QVEGE
QVEGT
QSOIL

You can inspect individual variables using:

ncks -m control.clm2.h0.0001-01.nc

docs.title: Useful Output Directories
docs.text:
Case setup:

$VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/cases/control

Run output:

$VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/output/control/run

Build logs:

$VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/output/control/bld

Archive files:

$VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/output/control/archive

docs.title: Part 7: Understanding the CESM Directory Structure
docs.text: A typical CESM installation contains four main directories.

docs.title: CESM Scripts Directory
docs.text:
Location:

cd $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/sources/ctsm-5.2.0/cime/scripts

Purpose:

Create new cases
Manage configurations
Generate case directories

Important command:

./create_newcase

docs.title: Input Data Directory
docs.text:
Location:

cd $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/inputdata

Purpose:

Meteorological forcing
Surface datasets
Domain files
Parameter files
Initial conditions

This directory is typically shared between users.

docs.title: Cases Directory
docs.text:
Location:

cd $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/cases

Purpose:

Case setup
Configuration
Compilation
Submission

Each simulation has its own case directory.

Example:

cases/control

docs.title: Output Directory
docs.text:
Location:

cd $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/output

Purpose:

Executable build files
Runtime files
Model output
Restart files
Logs

This is where the model actually runs.

docs.title: Useful Links
docs.text:
CLM5 namelists:

https://www.cesm.ucar.edu/models/cesm2/settings/2.2.0/clm5_0_nml.html

CESM2 compsets:

https://www.cesm.ucar.edu/models/cesm2/config/compsets.html

CLM5 documentation:

https://escomp.github.io/ctsm-docs/versions/release-clm5.0/html/tech_note/index.html

CESM website:

http://www.cesm.ucar.edu/models/cesm2/

Example plotting scripts:

https://github.com/sdeherto/CLM-FATES4UGent/tree/main/plotting

docs.title: Part 15: Further Resources and Getting Help
FATES Documentation
docs.text:
FATES User Guide:

https://fates-users-guide.readthedocs.io/

The documentation includes:

  • User Guide
  • Developer Guide
  • Technical Documentation
  • Tutorials

docs.title: FATES Discussions
docs.text:
GitHub Discussions:

https://github.com/NGEET/FATES/discussions

Recommended for:

  • Questions
  • Ideas
  • Announcements
  • Community support

docs.title: Reporting Bugs
docs.text:
GitHub Issues:

https://github.com/NGEET/FATES/issues

If you encounter a bug, provide:

FATES version
CTSM version
Error messages
Relevant namelists
Reproducible example

docs.title: FATES Community
docs.text:
Google Group:

https://groups.google.com/g/fates_model

The group provides:

  • Announcements
  • Model updates
  • Community discussions

docs.title: FATES Community Calls
docs.text:
Held approximately every two weeks.

Topics include:

  • New model developments
  • Scientific applications
  • User questions
  • Office hours

docs.title: CTSM Resources
docs.text:
Documentation:

https://escomp.github.io/ctsm-docs/versions/master/html/index.html

Discussions:

https://github.com/ESCOMP/CTSM/discussions

docs.title: E3SM Resources
docs.text:
Documentation:

https://docs.e3sm.org/E3SM/

Discussions:

https://github.com/E3SM-Project/E3SM/discussions

docs.title: CIME Resources
docs.text:
Documentation:

https://esmci.github.io/cime/versions/master/html/index.html

CIME controls:

Case creation
Configuration
Compilation
Submission
Workflow management

and is shared across multiple Earth System Models.

docs.title: Suggested Next Steps
docs.text:
After completing this tutorial, useful next projects include:

  1. Run a longer BCI simulation.
  2. Compare inventory initialization with bare-ground initialization.
  3. Create a custom two-PFT parameter file.
  4. Modify trait values and explore coexistence.
  5. Run a simulation for a new site.
  6. Generate custom meteorological forcing.
  7. Create a regional simulation.
  8. Analyse FATES outputs in Python or R.
  9. Compare simulations against field observations.
  10. Build your own FATES workflow for a research question.

docs.title: Useful Links
docs.text:
• CLM5 namelists

  • CESM2 compsets

  • CLM5 documentation

  • CESM website

  • Example plotting scripts

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
You will need:

  • A VSC account
  • Access to the Hydra cluster at VUB
  • CESM dependencies available through the module system

For UGent users:

  • Input data will be stored in shared UGent storage
  • Simulations will be compiled and executed on Hydra
  • Model output should be written to $VSC_SCRATCH, which is local to Hydra

docs.title: Load Required Modules
docs.text:
Load the CESM dependencies:

module load CESM-deps/2-foss-2024a

docs.title: Create the CESM Directory Structure
docs.text:
Create a working directory:

mkdir $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm

Create the standard CESM folder structure:

mkdir $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/cases
mkdir $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/output
mkdir $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/sources

Create a symbolic link to the shared CESM input data repository:

ln -sf /data/gent/vo/000/gvo00074/inputdata_cesm \
       $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/inputdata

The resulting directory structure should look like:

cesm/
├── cases/
├── output/
├── sources/
└── inputdata

docs.title: Download CTSM
docs.text:
Move to the sources directory:

cd $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/sources

Clone CTSM:

git clone -b ctsm5.2.0 https://github.com/ESCOMP/CTSM ctsm-5.2.0

docs.title: Download External Components
docs.text:
Move into the CTSM source directory:

cd ctsm-5.2.0

Download the required external components:

./manage_externals/checkout_externals

This step downloads all CESM components required by CTSM.

docs.title: Configure CESM for Hydra
docs.text:
Add the Hydra machine configuration to CESM:

update-cesm-machines -m ctsm $EBROOTCESMMINDEPS/machines/

This allows CESM to recognise Hydra as a supported machine for compilation and job submission.

docs.title: Part 10: Working with Inventory Initialization
docs.text: FATES supports several initialization modes.

docs.title: Restart Initialization
docs.text:
Continue from a previous simulation.

Requires:

Restart files

docs.title: Bare Ground Initialization
docs.text:
Start from an empty landscape.

Useful when:

No inventory data are available
Long spinup simulations are required

docs.title: Inventory Initialization
docs.text:
Initialize the simulation using field inventory data.

This starts the simulation with a realistic forest structure already present.

Inventory initialization requires:

Patch file (.pss)
Cohort file (.css)
Control file

docs.title: Inventory Files
docs.text: The inventory files describe:

docs.title: Patch Structure (.pss)
docs.text:
Defines:

Patch ages
Patch areas
Disturbance history

docs.title: Cohort Structure (.css)
docs.text:
Defines:

Tree sizes
Tree densities
Species composition

docs.title: Control File
docs.text:
Defines:

Location of inventory files
Site information

docs.title: Inventory Initialization Control File
docs.text:
The tutorial inventory setup uses:

vi $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/inventory/fates_bci_jra_inventory_ctrl

Check that the paths point to your inventory files.

Replace any hardcoded VSC numbers with your own.

docs.title: Switching to Bare Ground
docs.text:
If inventory initialization is unavailable:

use_fates_inventory_init = .false.

This starts the simulation from bare ground.

Inventory initialization is preferred whenever high-quality inventory data are available.

docs.title: Converting Field Data
docs.text:
To use your own inventory data, field observations must be converted into FATES inventory files.

Guidance is available here:

https://fates-users-guide.readthedocs.io/projects/tutorial/en/latest/inventory_data.html

This conversion step is site-specific and usually requires:

Species information
DBH measurements
Tree densities
Plot dimensions

docs.title: Part 13: Setting Up Regional Simulations
docs.text: Regional simulations work similarly to site-level simulations but cover many grid cells.

docs.title: Generate Regional Surface Data
docs.text:
Navigate to:

cd $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/sources/ctsm-5.2.0/tools/site_and_regional

Run:

./subset_data region \
    --create-surface \
    --lat1 -25 \
    --lat2 25 \
    --lon1 1 \
    --lon2 45 \
    --overwrite \
    --verbose \
    --outdir $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/sitedata/africa \
    --inputdata-dir $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/inputdata

docs.title: Create the Mesh File
docs.text:
Load NCO:

module load NCO

Copy the SCRIP file:

cp $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/sitedata/scrip.nc ./

Generate the mesh:

ncks --rgr infer --rgr scrip=scrip.nc \
<fsurdat_generated_in_previous_step> \
lnd_mesh.nc

Convert:

ESMF_Scrip2Unstruct scrip.nc lnd_mesh.nc 0

docs.title: Create a Regional Run Script
docs.text:
Start from:

create_clm-fates-africa.sh

Rename it for your region.

docs.title: Update the Script
docs.text:
Modify:

SITE
surface file
mesh file
STOP_N
REST_N

and any desired output variables.

Regional simulations are useful when:

A single site is too small
Continental-scale patterns are required
Spatially explicit vegetation dynamics are needed

docs.title: Part 8: Setting Up a Site-Level FATES Simulation
docs.text:
So far we have run a standard CLM-FATES simulation using global datasets.

Next we will run FATES at a single site using inventory and meteorological observations.

Site-level simulations allow direct comparison with field measurements such as:

  • Forest inventories
  • Flux tower measurements
  • Local meteorological observations
  • Trait measurements

docs.title: Example Site: Barro Colorado Island (BCI)
docs.text:
For this tutorial we will use:

Barro Colorado Island (BCI), Panama

The simulation uses:

Inventory data
Local meteorological forcing
FATES vegetation dynamics

docs.title: Creating a Parameter File Directory
docs.text:
Create a directory for custom parameter files:

mkdir $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/params

Move into it:

cd $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/params

docs.title: Copy the Default FATES Parameter File
docs.text:
Copy the default parameter file:

cp $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/inputdata/lnd/clm2/paramdata/fates_params_api.32.0.0_12pft_c231215.nc \
   fates_params_default.nc

docs.title: Converting Between NetCDF and CDL
docs.text:
FATES parameter files are stored as NetCDF files.

To inspect them more easily they can be converted into CDL format.

Convert NetCDF to CDL:

ncdump fates_params_default.nc > fates_params_default.cdl

Convert CDL back to NetCDF:

ncgen -o fates_params_default.nc fates_params_default.cdl

Verify the files exist:

ls

docs.title: Create a Single-PFT Parameter File
docs.text:
Copy the PFT manipulation script:

cp $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/sources/ctsm-5.2.0/src/fates/tools/FatesPFTIndexSwapper.py .

Load SciPy:

ml SciPy-bundle/2023.07-gfbf-2023a

Create a parameter file containing only a single tropical PFT:

./FatesPFTIndexSwapper.py \
    --fin=fates_params_default.nc \
    --fout=fates_params_default-1pft.nc \
    --pft-indices=1

This creates a simplified parameter file containing only one PFT.

The resulting file will be used in the site-level simulations described in the following sections.

docs.title: Part 9: Running a Site-Level FATES Simulation
docs.text:
The easiest way to run FATES is through a create script that automates all four CESM workflow steps:

  1. create_newcase
  2. case.setup
  3. case.build
  4. case.submit

Example create scripts are available in:

https://github.com/sdeherto/CLM-FATES4UGent/tree/main/runscripts

For this tutorial we will use:

create_clm-fates-bci_jra-init.sh

This script creates a FATES simulation for:

Barro Colorado Island (BCI)
Inventory initialization
JRA climate forcing

docs.title: Prepare Directories
docs.text:
Create directories for run scripts and site data:

cd $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm

mkdir runscripts
mkdir sitedata

docs.title: Download Tutorial Files
docs.text:
Clone the tutorial repository:

git clone https://github.com/sdeherto/CLM-FATES4UGent.git

Move into the repository:

cd CLM-FATES4UGent

Copy the required directories:

cp -r runscripts $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/runscripts
cp -r sitedata $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/sitedata

docs.title: Modify the Create Script
docs.text:
Move to the runscripts directory:

cd $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/runscripts

Open the script:

vi create_clm-fates-bci_jra-init.sh

Some paths contain a hardcoded VSC number.

Replace:

vsc46573

with your own VSC number wherever necessary.

docs.title: Run the Create Script
docs.text:
Make the script executable:

chmod u+x create_clm-fates-bci_jra-init.sh

Launch the simulation:

./create_clm-fates-bci_jra-init.sh

The script will automatically:

  • Create the case
  • Configure the case
  • Build the executable
  • Submit the run

You will see a large amount of CESM output as the model compiles.

docs.title: Monitor Progress
docs.text:
Check the queue:

squeue

When the job status becomes:

R

the simulation is actively running.

docs.title: Locate Output
docs.text:
Navigate to the run directory.

Example:

cd output/fates-tutorial-bci_jra-inventory_init.2024-08-30/run

List the files:

ls

You should begin to see monthly output files such as:

*.elm.h0.1900-01.nc

One file is produced for each output period.

docs.title: Part 12: Setting Up FATES at a New Site
docs.text:
A new site simulation requires three main inputs:

Surface file
Domain file
Climate forcing

docs.title: Surface Files
docs.text:
Contain information such as:

Land cover
Soil properties
Topography
Vegetation characteristics

docs.title: Domain Files
docs.text:
Define:

Spatial extent
Grid information

for the simulation.

docs.title: Climate Forcing
docs.text:
Typically contains:

Solar radiation
Precipitation
Temperature
Humidity
Wind

at six-hourly or finer resolution.

docs.title: Generate Site Files
docs.text:
Navigate to:

cd $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/sources/ctsm-5.2.0/tools/site_and_regional

Run:

./subset_data point \
    --lat 8.95 \
    --lon 280.2 \
    --site bci_subset \
    --create-surface \
    --create-datm \
    --create-domain \
    --dompft 1 \
    --overwrite \
    --datm-syr 2003 \
    --datm-eyr 2015 \
    --create-user-mods \
    --outdir $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/sitedata/bci_test \
    --inputdata-dir $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/inputdata

This generates site-level files that can be used in a new FATES simulation.

docs.title: Create a New Site Script
docs.text:
Start from an existing example.

For example:

create_clm-fates-yangambi.sh

Rename it for your site.

docs.title: Update the Script
docs.text:
Modify:

SITE
PTS_LAT
PTS_LON
surface file
domain file
STOP_N
REST_N

Ensure the script points to the files generated by subset_data.

docs.title: Additional Output Variables
docs.text:
Extra output variables can be requested through:

user_nl_elm

within the create script.

docs.title: Part 13: Setting Up Regional Simulations
docs.text: Regional simulations work similarly to site-level simulations but cover many grid cells.

docs.title: Generate Regional Surface Data
docs.text:
Navigate to:

cd $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/sources/ctsm-5.2.0/tools/site_and_regional

Run:

./subset_data region \
    --create-surface \
    --lat1 -25 \
    --lat2 25 \
    --lon1 1 \
    --lon2 45 \
    --overwrite \
    --verbose \
    --outdir $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/sitedata/africa \
    --inputdata-dir $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/inputdata

docs.title: Create the Mesh File
docs.text:
Load NCO:

module load NCO

Copy the SCRIP file:

cp $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/sitedata/scrip.nc ./

Generate the mesh:

ncks --rgr infer --rgr scrip=scrip.nc \
<fsurdat_generated_in_previous_step> \
lnd_mesh.nc

Convert:

ESMF_Scrip2Unstruct scrip.nc lnd_mesh.nc 0

docs.title: Create a Regional Run Script
docs.text:
Start from:

create_clm-fates-africa.sh

Rename it for your region.

docs.title: Update the Script
docs.text:
Modify:

SITE
surface file
mesh file
STOP_N
REST_N

and any desired output variables.

Regional simulations are useful when:

A single site is too small
Continental-scale patterns are required
Spatially explicit vegetation dynamics are needed

docs.title: Part 14: Visualising Results
docs.text:
FATES typically writes one NetCDF output file per month.

Before analysis it is often useful to concatenate them into a single file.

docs.title: Move to the Run Directory
docs.text:
Example:

cd output/fates-tutorial-bci_jra-inventory_init.2024-08-30/run

docs.title: Concatenate Monthly Files
docs.text:
Use NCO:

ncrcat -h \
fates-tutorial-bci_jra-inventory_init.2024-08-30.elm.h0.190*.nc \
fates-tutorial-bci_jra-inventory_init.2024-08-30.sofar.nc

This command combines all monthly files into a single NetCDF file.

docs.title: Inspect the Output
docs.text:
View metadata:

ncdump -h fates-tutorial-bci_jra-inventory_init.2024-08-30.sofar.nc

View dimensions and variables:

ncks -m fates-tutorial-bci_jra-inventory_init.2024-08-30.sofar.nc

Visualise interactively:

ncview fates-tutorial-bci_jra-inventory_init.2024-08-30.sofar.nc

docs.title: Plotting Tutorials
docs.text:
Example analysis notebooks are available from the FATES tutorial:

https://github.com/NGEET/fates-tutorial/tree/main/fates-tutorial-jupyter-book

Both:

Python
R

versions are available.

These provide examples for:

  • Biomass trajectories
  • Basal area
  • PFT composition
  • Mortality
  • Recruitment
  • Carbon fluxes

docs.title: Example Plotting Scripts
docs.text:
Additional plotting scripts are available at:

https://github.com/sdeherto/CLM-FATES4UGent/tree/main/plotting

These can be used as a starting point for analysing your own simulations.

docs.title: Part 12: Modifying the FATES Parameter File
docs.text: One of the strengths of FATES is the ability to experiment with different plant functional types (PFTs) and trait combinations.

docs.title: Create a Two-PFT Parameter File
docs.text:
Move to the parameter directory:

cd $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/params

Create a new parameter file containing two identical copies of the same PFT:

./FatesPFTIndexSwapper.py \
    --fin=fates_params_default.nc \
    --fout=fates_params-2pfts.nc \
    --pft-indices=1,1

docs.title: Convert to CDL
docs.text:
Convert to a human-readable format:

ncdump fates_params-2pfts.nc > fates_params-2pfts.cdl

Open the file:

vi fates_params-2pfts.cdl

You can now modify parameters for each PFT independently.

docs.title: Rebuild the NetCDF File
docs.text:
After editing:

ncgen -o fates_params-2pfts.nc fates_params-2pfts.cdl

This step is mandatory.

FATES can only read the NetCDF version.

docs.title: Using modify_fates_paramfile.py
docs.text:
Copy the helper script:

cp $VSC_SCRATCH/cesm/sources/ctsm-5.2.0/src/fates/tools/modify_fates_paramfile.py .

The script supports:

--var   parameter name
--pft   PFT index
--fin   input file
--fout  output file
--val   new value
--O     overwrite

Example:

./modify_fates_paramfile.py \
    --var <parameter> \
    --pft 2 \
    --fin input.nc \
    --fout output.nc \
    --val <value> \
    --O

docs.title: Comparing Different Trait Strategies
docs.text:
A useful exercise is to create:

Early successional PFT
Late successional PFT

by modifying parameters related to:

  • Growth
  • Mortality
  • Wood density
  • Recruitment
  • Leaf economics

and comparing their coexistence through time.
